content_markdown:
A student blows across the top of a cylinder that contains water. A first-harmonic standing sound wave is produced in the air of the cylinder. More water is then added to the cylinder. The student blows so that a first-harmonic standing wave is produced with a different frequency.

What is the nature of the displacement in the air at the water surface and the change in frequency when the water is added?

diagram_metadata.description: Two diagrams of a cylinder containing water. The first shows a lower water level, and the second shows a higher water level after more water is added.

options.label: A
options.text: Nature of displacement: antinode; Change in frequency: decrease

options.label: B
options.text: Nature of displacement: antinode; Change in frequency: increase

options.label: C
options.text: Nature of displacement: node; Change in frequency: decrease

options.label: D
options.text: Nature of displacement: node; Change in frequency: increase

vibe_explanation: At a fixed boundary like a water surface, air molecules cannot move, so there must be a displacement node. For a pipe closed at one end, the fundamental frequency is given by $f = \frac{v}{4L}$. When water is added, the length $L$ of the air column decreases. Since frequency is inversely proportional to length, the frequency must increase.
